segments.purgatorio-19-g36: A single punctuation mark (a comma) separating the previous speech or clause from what follows; no words to analyze here.
segments.purgatorio-19-g37: fieramente (adverb) modifies the verb dicea and means 'fiercely' or 'harshly'; dicea is the imperfect tense (third person singular) of dire, used here as a past continuous 'was saying' (Dante frequently uses imperfect narration).
segments.purgatorio-19-g38: ed = e 'and' joining clauses; el is the archaic subject clitic for 'he'; venìa is the imperfect of venire, 'was coming' or 'approached' (past continuous); con introduces the manner 'with'; li occhi is the definite plural 'the eyes'; fitti is an adjectival participle meaning 'fixed, fastened' (an older/poetic form); pur = 'still' or 'even'; in = 'on/in'; quella onesta = 'that virtuous/honourable (woman)'. The whole phrase describes his approach while keeping his gaze fixed on that woman.
segments.purgatorio-19-g39: L’altra = 'the other (one)' with the masculine/feminine article clitic L' + altra 'other/another'; prendea is the imperfect of prendere, 'was seizing' or 'kept taking', used for an ongoing or repeated past action.
segments.purgatorio-19-g40: e = 'and'; dinanzi = 'before/in front (of)' (here 'before him/in front of me'); l’ is the clitic object 'her/it' (the preceding object); apria is an older imperfect form of aprire (here describing the repeated or simultaneous action 'would open' / 'opened'), used narratively; fendendo is the present participle of fendere 'splitting/cutting through' and modifies apria; i drappi = 'the draperies/cloths'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g41: e = 'and'; mostravami is the imperfect of mostrare with the enclitic -mi 'to me' (literally 'was showing to me'); the enclitic is attached to the verb in the usual medieval/poetic fashion; ’l = 'the' (the thing shown); ventre = 'belly' (literal body part).
segments.purgatorio-19-g42: quel = 'that' or 'that one' (referring back to what was shown); mi = indirect object/accusative 'me'; svegliò is the perfect/past simple (passato remoto) of svegliare 'awoke (someone)'; col = contraction of con + il 'with the'; puzzo = 'stench'; che = relative pronoun 'which/that'; n’ here is the clitic shorthand for 'ne' meaning 'from it' or 'of it' (poetic contraction) and uscia is an archaic form of uscire/usciva 'came out'. The clause states that that thing awakened the speaker by the stench that came from it.
segments.purgatorio-19-g43: Io 'I' is the subject; mossi is the passato remoto (first/third singular form) of muovere here meaning 'I moved' (a completed, punctual action); li occhi 'the eyes' is the direct object, so 'I turned my eyes' or 'I moved my eyes.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g44: e = 'and' linking the narration to the following utterance; ’l buon maestro = 'the good master' (the article clitic + adjective + noun), an address/title for Virgil; the colon introduces his speech.
segments.purgatorio-19-g45: Almen = archaic form of almeno 'at least'; tre voci = 'three voices/calls' (voci here = calls or utterances); t’ (ti) is the clitic object 'to you'; ho is the auxiliary 'I have'; messe is the past participle of mettere agreeing in gender/number with voci (feminine plural), a result of the preceding clitic construction — so literally 'I have put/put forth three calls to you', i.e. 'I've called you three times.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g46: A single comma marking the continuation of the narrator's line and the introduction of reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-19-g47: dicea is the imperfect of dire, 'was saying' (narrative imperfect), indicating the speaker's ongoing speech as he addressed Dante.
segments.purgatorio-19-g48: Surgi is the second-person singular imperative (or present subjunctive used as an imperative) of sorgere 'rise/get up' meaning 'rise!'; e = 'and'; vieni is the second-person singular imperative of venire 'come!'. Both are direct commands.
segments.purgatorio-19-g49: troviam is an archaic/metrical form of troviamo (first-person plural present) meaning 'we find' or hortatively 'let us find'; l’aperta = 'the opening' (l' + aperta, feminine); per = 'through' or 'by means of'; la qual = 'which' (the relative pronoun agreeing with 'la aperta'); tu = 'you'; entre is an older or dialectal form of entri/entrare 'enter' (here 'you enter'); the clause means 'we will find the opening through which you will enter.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g26: Io is the explicit subject pronoun 'I'. volsi is the passato remoto (simple past) of volgere/volgere meaning 'I turned' and takes a direct object (Ulisse). del = di + il here marks origin/starting point ('from the'); suo cammin is 'his path/journey' with the adjective vago ('wandering') placed after cammin as a poetic adjective. al canto mio = a + il 'to the' + 'my song' (direction/toward).
segments.purgatorio-19-g27: e is the coordinating 'and'. qual is an archaic/poetic form of quale here functioning like 'whoever'/'such as (who)'. meco is the old-fashioned compound prepositional phrase meaning 'with me'. s’ausa is si + ausa (ausa = 'dares, ventures'); s’ is the clitic reflexive pronoun attached to the verb and ausa is present tense third person, giving a general present 'dares (to himself)'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g28: rado is an adverb meaning 'seldom/rarely'. sen is an enclitic form (se + ne / se n') meaning 'away/from it' and is placed before the verb here. parte is from partire/'to part/leave' used in the third-person sense 'parts/leaves' (in the text it appears as the form parte). Together rado sen parte = 'seldom parts from it/rarely withdraws.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g29: sì here means 'so/thus' (giving a result). tutto modifies the following noun/adverbial idea and here has the force 'entirely/completely'. l' is the elided definite article (lo) before appago. appago in this context is a noun/abstract derived from appagare meaning 'satisfaction/fulfillment' or 'that which pays/repays', so the phrase claims 'thus I wholly satisfy/pay off (him/it)'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g30: Single punctuation mark: a full stop ending the preceding exclamation. No lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-19-g31: Ancor (archaic form of ancora) 'still/yet' precedes the negation non. era is the imperfect (past) of essere 'was' describing a past state. sua bocca is 'his mouth' with the possessive before the noun, and richiusa is a past participle used adjectivally 'closed again/reclosed.' The clause means 'his mouth was not yet re-closed.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g32: quand' = quando 'when'. una donna apparve uses apparve (passato remoto) 'appeared' in the third person. santa e presta are coordinated adjectives 'holy and ready/quick' (presta = ready/quick in archaic use). lunghesso is an archaic adverb meaning roughly 'meanwhile' or 'shortly thereafter' and me is the indirect object 'to me' (the woman appeared to me). per far colei confusa is a purpose clause: per + infinitive far 'to make', colei 'that one/she', confusa past participle used adjectivally 'confused/abashed.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g33: O is the vocative/exclamatory 'Oh', used here to address the person directly. Virgilio is the vocative form of the proper name 'Virgil.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g34: A repetition of the vocative name for emphasis. The single word Virgilio is again direct address.
segments.purgatorio-19-g35: chi is the interrogative 'who'. è is the present indicative of essere 'is'. questa is the demonstrative 'this (one)', referring to the woman just mentioned; together chi è questa? = 'who is this?'
segments.purgatorio-19-g91: O is the vocative interjection (old/poetic "oh"). eletti is a plural substantive used vocatively meaning "the elect/chosen". di Dio is a simple prepositional phrase "of God," with di + proper noun.
segments.purgatorio-19-g92: li cui is a classical/medieval relative construction meaning "those whose" (li = plural article + cui = whose). soffriri is a noun (abstract plural) here "sufferings." e ... e links two causes: "both justice and hope." giustizia and speranza are nouns; fa is present tense of fare used causatively ("make"). men is an adverbial/poetic form for "less" and duri is predicate adjective "hard/harsh."
segments.purgatorio-19-g93: drizzate is the 2nd-person-plural imperative of drizzare meaning "direct/guide/straighten" (a request to the addressed). noi is the object pronoun "us." verso = "toward." li alti saliri: li is the plural article, alti is adjective "lofty/high," and saliri (archaic form) functions as a masculine plural noun "ascents/going up."
segments.purgatorio-19-g94: Se here introduces a conditional clause ("if"); voi venite is present indicative with voi as subject "you come/arrive." dal = da + il = "from the," and giacer is an infinitive/nominal form meaning "to lie" (here 'lying' = bed/rest). sicuri is a predicative adjective agreeing in plural with voi meaning "safe/secure/certain."
segments.purgatorio-19-g95: e links the clauses. volete is 2nd-person-plural present of volere "you wish/want." trovar is the infinitive "to find," so volete + infinitive expresses desire. la via = "the way/path." più tosto is a comparative adverbial phrase meaning "more quickly/sooner" (più modifies tosto).
segments.purgatorio-19-g96: le vostre destre = "your right hands/sides" (le + possessive + plural noun). sien is the present subjunctive (from essere) used optatively/jussively here — "may be" / "let them be." sempre = "always." di fori is an archaic/prepositional phrase meaning "outside" (fori is older form related to fuori).
segments.purgatorio-19-g97: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' and sits before the finite verb pregò for emphasis; pregò is the passato remoto (simple past) of pregare meaning 'implored/asked' (an acted request), and ’l poeta is the inverted/article + noun 'the poet' (poeta is the subject).
segments.purgatorio-19-g98: e is the coordinating 'and'; sì modifies risposto and means 'thus' (i.e., 'an answer thus'); risposto is a past participle used impersonally here ('an answer was given'); poco dinanzi a noi = 'shortly before us' (poco + dinanzi + a noi); ne is the partitive/relational clitic 'of it/thereof' tied to fu; fu is passato remoto of essere 'was'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g99: per ch’io is a fused causal/temporal connector ('wherefore/so that I'); nel parlare is a prepositional phrase 'in speaking' (nel = in + il); avvisai is passato remoto 'I informed/warned'; l’altro nascosto is the direct object 'the other, hidden'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g100: e 'and' connects clauses; volsi is passato remoto of volgere 'I turned'; li occhi 'the eyes' (li = definite article plural) followed by a li occhi 'to the eyes' (repetition stresses direction) and al segnor mio = 'to my lord' (al = a + il). The sequence places the direct object (occhi) before the directional phrase.
segments.purgatorio-19-g101: ond’ (ondè/onde) is an enclitic/contraction meaning 'whereupon/therefore'; elli here is the pronoun 'he' (not listed correctly in the provided senses); m’assentì is mi + assentì, passato remoto 'he consented/agreed to me'; con lieto cenno is the instrumental phrase 'with a cheerful/happy nod'; ciò che introduces a relative clause 'that which'; chiedea is imperfect 'was asking/requesting' and la vista del disio is 'the sight of longing/desire' (vista = sight; del = of the; disio = longing).
segments.purgatorio-19-g102: Poi ch’io is a temporal concessive 'after I...' (poi che); potei is passato remoto 'I was able'; di me fare a mio senno is an idiomatic reflexive-like construction 'to make myself to my senses' (fare + di me + a mio senno = restore one's reason).
segments.purgatorio-19-g103: trassimi is an archaic past (from trarre) meaning 'drew me' or 'pulled me up' (1st person past); sovra quella creatura = 'upon that creature' (sovra = on/upon); le cui parole pria notar mi fenno is a relative clause 'whose words earlier made me take notice' (fenno = fecero/made, notar = note/observe, mi is indirect object). Note: creatura in sense 'creature' is the expected reading.
segments.purgatorio-19-g104: dicendo is the present participle 'saying,' introducing direct speech that follows; grammatically it attaches to the action of the creature that drew Dante up.
segments.purgatorio-19-g105: This is the beginning of the direct address and relative constructions: Spirto 'Spirit' is the vocative; in cui introduces a relative 'in whom' governing the subordinate clause; pianger matura uses matura (present) with an impersonal/figurative sense 'it is fitting/mature to weep' (matura + infinitive = 'it is proper to...'); quel sanza ’l quale a Dio tornar non pòssi = 'that one without whom you cannot return to God' (sanza = senza; note archaic spelling and that pòssi is an older form whose person here functions as 'you cannot' in context).
segments.purgatorio-19-g106: sosta is the imperative or present subjunctive used as a command 'stop/stay' addressed to the spirit; un poco 'a little'; per me tua maggior cura literally 'for me, your greater concern' i.e., 'for my greater care of you' (per = for; tua maggior cura = your greater care / my greater concern for you), so this clause gives the reason for pausing.
segments.purgatorio-19-g118: A feminine noun phrase: the article + possessive (La mia) agrees with conversïone (feminine singular). Conversïone is an archaic orthography (the dieresis marks the vowel separation) meaning a spiritual conversion; the whole phrase is the direct topic/subject being lamented.
segments.purgatorio-19-g119: Omè is an interjection meaning 'alas' or 'ah me', expressing lament; it stands alone and addresses the speaker's regret about the preceding noun phrase.
segments.purgatorio-19-g120: A comma marking the pause between clauses; it separates the exclamation from the following narrative sentence.
segments.purgatorio-19-g121: Fu is the passato remoto (past historic) of essere, here with the predicate adjective tarda ('late', feminine) agreeing implicitly with 'la mia conversïone' (the subject is omitted but understood). The phrase means 'it/that was late.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g122: Ma is the simple coordinating conjunction 'but', introducing a contrastive clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-19-g123: Come here functions temporally as 'when' or 'as'; fatto is a past participle used with fui ('I was') in an idiomatic periphrasis meaning 'I became' or 'I had become'; Roman pastore is the predicate noun phrase ('a Roman shepherd'). The entire clause means 'when I became a Roman shepherd.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g124: Così 'thus' links the previous temporal clause to the result; scopersi is passato remoto of scoprirsi (here used reflexively) meaning 'I discovered' or 'I found (that)'; la vita bugiarda is the object 'the deceitful life' (vita = way of life).
segments.purgatorio-19-g125: Vidi is passato remoto 'I saw'; che introduces an indirect statement; lì 'there' is a locative adverb; non ... s'acquetava is a negated imperfect reflexive verb (s'acquetava = 'calmed itself' / 'was pacified') in third person singular; il core is the subject 'the heart'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g126: Né 'nor' continues the negative idea; più here means 'any longer'; salir is the infinitive 'to ascend'; potiesi is an archaic past form of potere meaning 'was able' (so 'could'); the clause means 'nor could I climb any more in that life' with in quella vita specifying the domain.
segments.purgatorio-19-g127: Per che functions as a consequence marker 'therefore/so'; di questa 'about/of this' refers back to the just-mentioned life; in me 'within me'; s'accese is si accese (reflexive) passato remoto of accendersi meaning 'was kindled' or 'was lit'; amore is the subject that was kindled — 'love was kindled in me for this.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g128: Fino a quel punto = 'up to that point'; misera e partita da Dio anima fui is a predicate describing the speaker: misera (wretched) and partita da Dio (departed/separated from God) modify anima (soul). Fui is passato remoto 'I was' and the past participle partita agrees with anima (feminine).
segments.purgatorio-19-g129: Del tutto is an adverbial phrase 'completely' (literally 'of the whole') modifying avara ('miserly/greedy'); the semicolon ends the clause and introduces the contrasting present situation.
segments.purgatorio-19-g130: Or is a discourse adverb meaning 'now' or 'at present', introducing the ensuing comment.
segments.purgatorio-19-g131: Come vedi = 'as you see' or 'as you perceive'; come is 'as', vedi is present second-person singular of vedere ('you see'), directly addressing the listener/reader.
segments.purgatorio-19-g132: Qui 'here' indicates place/situation; ne is a pronominal particle referring back to the cause ('of it'/'for that'); son is present of essere ('I am'); punita is a past participle used predicatively ('punished') and agrees in gender with anima — 'here I am punished for it.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g1: Ne l’ora che ... is an adverbial phrase meaning “in the hour when.” Ne = in (archaic/prepositional use with time), l’ora = the hour, che introduces a subordinate clause ‘when.’ Non può ... intepidar più is a negated modal construction: non + può (present, ‘cannot’) + infinitive intepidar (‘to make warm/slightly warm’); più here means ‘any longer’ (‘no longer’). Freddo de la luna is a genitive expression ‘the cold of the moon.’ Note the archaic diaeresis in dïurno marking syllable division.
segments.purgatorio-19-g2: Vinto is a past participle used adjectivally: 'overcome/subdued.' The short phrase 'vinto da terra' is parenthetical and uses da to mark the agent or cause ('by/from the earth'), so it qualifies the preceding idea (the moon’s cold being subdued by the earth).
segments.purgatorio-19-g3: E talor da Saturno continues the parenthetical idea: talor = sometimes, da Saturno = 'by Saturn' (astrological/planetary influence). The following subordinate quando introduces an explanatory temporal clause: i geomanti (geomancers/diviners) + lor Maggior Fortuna ('their Greater Fortune') + veggiono (they see/foresee) in orïente (in the East/dawn). Lor = their modifies 'Maggior Fortuna.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g4: Innanzi a l’alba = 'before dawn.' Innanzi is a preposition meaning 'before/in front of' and combines with a + l’alba to form the temporal phrase 'before dawn.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g5: Surger is an archaic infinitive (to rise/arise) used in the clause describing what the 'Maggior Fortuna' does; per via = 'along/by a road/way.' Che introduces a relative clause modifying via: poco = 'little/slightly' and le is a dative pronoun referring back to Fortuna ('to it/her'); sta bruna = 'stands/appears dark/gloomy' (sta = 'is/stands'). The overall sense: it rises along a path which soon appears/dawns dim to it.
segments.purgatorio-19-g6: Mi venne in sogno = 'came to me in a dream.' Venne is passato remoto (simple past) 'came'; mi is dative 'to me'; in sogno is the adverbial phrase 'in a dream/vision.' Una femmina balba = 'a stammering woman' (balba = stammering).
segments.purgatorio-19-g7: Ne li occhi = 'in the eyes' (ne = in/of them here used with body-part phrase), guercia is an adjective meaning 'squinting' or 'cross-eyed.' Word order is compact: 'in the eyes squinting.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g8: E sovra i piè distorta = 'and with her feet twisted.' Sovra = 'over/on/above' used here with body-part phrase; i piè = the feet; distorta is an adjective 'twisted/distorted.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g9: Con le man monche = 'with hands maimed/shortened.' Con = with, le man = the hands (man = mano), monche = adjective 'maimed/truncated.' This is a simple prepositional phrase describing her condition.
segments.purgatorio-19-g10: E di colore scialba = 'and of a sallow/pale color.' Di colore is a genitive-like descriptive phrase 'of color/complexion' and scialba = 'pale/sallow.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g11: Io la mirava = subject + object + imperfect verb. Io = 'I' (explicit subject), la = direct object 'her,' mirava is the imperfect of mirare 'to gaze at' meaning 'I was gazing at / I gazed at.' The imperfect emphasizes an ongoing action.
segments.purgatorio-19-g12: E come ’l sol conforta ... is a simile introduced by come = 'as.' ’l sol = 'the sun' (poetic article plus sol), conforta (present) = 'comforts/warms,' and the relative clause le fredde membra che la notte aggrava = 'the cold limbs which the night burdens/presses down.' Che introduces the relative clause; aggrava is present (or present-tense narrative) 'makes heavier/worse.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g13: Così lo sguardo mio le facea scorta la lingua = inversion for emphasis. Literally 'thus my gaze made the tongue an escort for her': lo sguardo mio = 'my look' (lo is the article), le = dative 'to her,' facea scorta = 'made escort/provided as a guide,' and la lingua = 'the tongue/speech' (object placed at end). The phrasing means my look gave her the power of speech or guided her speech.
segments.purgatorio-19-g14: E poscia tutta la drizzava in poco d’ora = 'and afterwards it straightened her completely in a short while.' Poscia = afterwards, tutta = completely, la = her, drizzava = imperfect of drizzare 'to straighten/raise up,' in poco d'ora = 'in a little time/short while.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g15: E lo smarrito volto = 'and the bewildered face.' Lo is the article, smarrito is an adjective ('lost/bewildered'), volto here = 'face' (literally 'turned [one]'). The phrase stands as the object that is then colored.
segments.purgatorio-19-g16: Com’ amor vuol = 'as love wishes/wills.' Com’ is a contraction of come, amor = 'love' (personified), vuol is third-person singular present of volere 'wants/commands.' This phrase qualifies how the face was colored.
segments.purgatorio-19-g17: Così le colorava = 'thus it colored her.' Così = thus, le = dative 'her,' colorava = imperfect of colorare 'colored/dyed' (here 'gave color to'). The imperfect continues the descriptive, habitual narrative tone.
segments.purgatorio-19-g18: Poi ch’ell’ avea ’l parlar così disciolto = temporal clause 'after that she had her speech thus loosened.' Poi ch' = 'after that/since,' ell' = she, avea (imperfect) + past participle disciolto expresses a prior state ('had loosened/been loosened'), l parlar = 'the speech' (parlar = noun 'speech/talking'), così = thus. The participle disciolto means 'loosened/freed' (her speech was fluent).
segments.purgatorio-19-g19: Cominciava a cantar sì = 'she began to sing so (much/so well).' Cominciava = imperfect 'began' or 'was beginning,' a + infinitive cantar = to sing, sì here intensifies the manner 'so/thus.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g20: Che con pena da lei avrei mio intento rivolto = subordinate result clause introduced by che = 'that/so that.' Con pena = 'with difficulty' (pena here = effort/difficulty), da lei = 'because of her/by her' (indicating cause), avrei ... rivolto = conditional perfect 'I would have turned' (avrei = conditional auxiliary + participle rivolto = turned/toward), mio intento = 'my attention/intent.' The clause means it would have been with difficulty that I could have turned my attention away from her.
segments.purgatorio-19-g21: «Io son», = simple declarative 'I am.' Io = I (subject), son = present of essere 'am' (poetic/archaic form).
segments.purgatorio-19-g22: Cantava = imperfect 'she was singing' or 'she sang' as a habitual/immediate narrative action; marks that the words that follow are sung.
segments.purgatorio-19-g23: «io son dolce serena, = 'I am sweet/ gentle serenity.' Io son = 'I am,' dolce = 'sweet/gentle,' serena = adjective/noun 'serene/calm' (here personified as the speaker's identity).
segments.purgatorio-19-g24: Che ’ marinari in mezzo mar dismago; = relative clause 'who relieves mariners in mid-sea of distress.' Che = who/that, marinari = mariners (here plural noun; poetic morphology), in mezzo mar = 'in the middle of the sea,' dismago = 'distress/vexation' (object of the implied verb 'free/relieve').
segments.purgatorio-19-g25: Tanto son di piacere a sentir piena! = 'So full of pleasure am I to be heard!' Tanto = so/so much, son = 'am,' di piacere = 'of pleasure' (di + noun describes the quality), a sentir = 'to be heard' / 'to hear' (infinitive used impersonally here: pleasurable to be listened to), piena = full (agreeing with implied feminine speaker).
segments.purgatorio-19-g76: Bastiti is an archaic/poetic second-person plural command (imperative) formed from bastire with a reflexive sense: "equip/arm yourselves" or "make yourselves ready." The verb is addressed to the companions and stands alone as an imperative directive.
segments.purgatorio-19-g77: This is a coordinated imperative clause: e (and) links to the previous command; batti is a second-person singular imperative (stamp/strike); a terra is the prepositional phrase (onto/against the ground); le calcagne is the direct object (the heels). Word order is normal for an imperative with its object after the verb.
segments.purgatorio-19-g78: Li occhi (the eyes) is the object phrase; rivolgi is a second-person singular imperative meaning "turn (your)." Al logoro is a prepositional phrase "to the logoro" (see note on sense); che gira is a relative clause modifying logoro ("which turns"); lo rege etterno is the subject of gira ("the eternal King"); con le rote magne is an instrumental/associative phrase "with the mighty wheels." The clauses link by imperative instruction to look toward that moving object.
segments.purgatorio-19-g79: Quale here introduces a simile: "as" or "such as." ’l falcon is the simile's subject (the falcon) with archaic elided article (’l = il).
segments.purgatorio-19-g80: This relative clause (che ... si mira) modifies falcon: che = "which/that"; prima = "first/at first"; a’ pié = contraction of a + i/e article + pié = "at (his) feet"; si mira is a reflexive present indicating the bird "looks/observes itself/at the ground"—here "watches at his feet."
segments.purgatorio-19-g81: indi (an adverb meaning "then/thereupon") begins the sequence (note: the provided sense list lacks this adverb); si volge = reflexive "turns himself/turns toward"; al grido = "to the cry/call"; e si protende = "and stretches himself forth" (reflexive again); per lo disio del pasto = "because of the longing for the prey/meal" (per indicates motive); che là il tira = relative clause "which draws him there" (il = him).
segments.purgatorio-19-g82: tal mi fec’ io is an independent clause: tal = "so/thus"; mi = indirect object "to me" (here "made me so" in effect); fec’ is the contracted past (feci) meaning "made/caused" and the final io is the emphatic subject "I." The clause means "so I did/made myself so."
segments.purgatorio-19-g83: A short connective: e = "and"; tal = "such/so" preparing the comparison continued in the following clause.
segments.purgatorio-19-g84: Quanto here introduces a comparison "in the measure that/as much as"; si fende = passive/reflexive present "is split/cleaves"; la roccia = "the rock"; per dar via = purpose phrase "to give way" (per + infinitive = in order to); a chi va suso = "to him who goes up" (chi = "whoever/one who"). The clause compares degree of the speaker's motion to how a rock gives way to one climbing up.
segments.purgatorio-19-g85: n’andai is a verbal form where n’ (ne/from there) is an enclitic adverbial element joined to andai (I went); infin = "as far as/until"; dove = "where"; ’l cerchiar = "the circling" (article + noun, archaic elision); si prende = reflexive "takes hold/is taken/begins." The clause means "I went on as far as where the circling begins/takes hold."
segments.purgatorio-19-g86: Com’ = short for come, "as/when" introducing the temporal clause; io = subject "I"; nel quinto giro = "in/into the fifth circuit" (nel = in + il); fui dischiuso = perfect passive "I was opened/let out" (fui + past participle dischiuso).
segments.purgatorio-19-g87: vidi = simple past "I saw"; gente = "people"; per esso = "along/through it" (here indicating 'on that circle'); che piangea = relative clause "who were weeping" (piangea = imperfect tense, ongoing past action).
segments.purgatorio-19-g88: giacendo = present participle "lying" describing their posture; a terra = "on the ground"; tutta volta in giuso = "wholly/entirely turned downward" (tutta qualifies volta, volta = turned/turned-over, in giuso = downward). The participial clause gives the manner in which they lay.
segments.purgatorio-19-g89: A quoted Latin line (Adhaesit pavimento anima mea) is followed by sentia dir lor: sentia (imperf. of sentire) means "I heard" or "I perceived"; dir = infinitive "say" used with sentia to mark what was heard; lor = dative "to them" (or "them" as indirect object). Con sì alti sospiri is an accompanying adverbial phrase: con = "with," sì = "so/such," alti sospiri = "lofty/deep sighs."
segments.purgatorio-19-g90: che introduces a result clause: la parola = "the word/words"; a pena = idiom meaning "with difficulty/barely" (a + pena); s’ intendea = impersonal/reflexive imperfect of intendere meaning "was understood/one could make out" (s’ = si). The clause states that the spoken words were scarcely intelligible because of the sighing.
segments.purgatorio-19-g152: Punctuation-only segment: a single period marking a pause/end of line; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-19-g153: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and' introducing the speaker; io is the stressed subject pronoun 'I'; a lui is an indirect-object phrase 'to him' used to introduce direct speech (hence the colon). Word order is neutral: subject + prepositional indirect object.
segments.purgatorio-19-g154: Per introduces a reason 'because of/for'; vostra dignitate is the cause phrase 'your dignity' (vostra agrees in gender/number with dignitate). Mia coscïenza is the grammatical subject of rimorse; dritto is an adverb here meaning 'justly' or 'straight/directly', modifying the verb rimorse; mi is an indirect object/clitic 'me'; rimorse is a past tense (passato remoto) form meaning 'it caused remorse/it reproved' (here 'my conscience reproached me').
segments.purgatorio-19-g155: Drizza is the second-person singular imperative of drizzare 'straighten/raise/direct' commanding action; le gambe is the direct-object phrase 'the legs' (Italian commonly uses the definite article with body parts where English uses a possessive).
segments.purgatorio-19-g156: Lèvati is the second-person singular imperative with clitic -ti (reflexive), literally 'raise yourself' or 'get up'; sù is an adverb 'up' reinforcing the motion upward. The reflexive form indicates the subject performs the action on himself.
segments.purgatorio-19-g157: Frate is a vocative 'brother' (address to a fellow monk or person), an older/poetic form equivalent to moderno 'fratello' or 'fra'. It functions as an appeal or form of address following the commands.
segments.purgatorio-19-g158: A punctuation segment containing only a comma; it links the reported speech to the narrator's action and marks a pause before the verb of speaking.
segments.purgatorio-19-g159: Rispuose is the passato remoto (simple past) third-person singular of rispondere 'to answer', meaning 'he answered' or 'replied'. The semicolon in the original separates the narrative verb from the following quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-19-g160: Non is the standard negation 'not'; errar is an archaic infinitive form of errare 'to err/wander'. Here the infinitive is used in a negative exhortation sense equivalent to a negative imperative 'do not err' (an older, poetic usage instead of a modern imperative form).
segments.purgatorio-19-g161: Conservo sono shows poetic inversion: the noun/concept conservo (here functioning as a substantive meaning 'guardian/keeper' or one who 'keeps/guards') comes before the verb sono 'I am' for emphasis — effectively 'I am a conservator/guardian'. Teco 'with you' and e con li altri 'and with the others' are prepositional phrases indicating company; ad una podestate means 'under one authority' or 'to one power/office', with ad + una indicating subjection or jurisdiction.
segments.purgatorio-19-g50: Sù is an adverb meaning 'up' placed before the verb for emphasis. Mi is the clitic reflexive/object pronoun marking the pronominal verb levarsi; levai is passato remoto, first person singular of levarsi, so mi levai = 'I rose (up)'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g51: This line shows an inverted word order: subject complement ('tutti' = all) and predicate (eran già pieni = were already full) precede the prepositional phrase that names what they are full of. 'De l’alto dì' = 'of the high/daylight', and the postponed noun phrase 'i giron del sacro monte' is the logical subject ('the circuits/rounds of the sacred mountain').
segments.purgatorio-19-g52: Andavam is the imperfect tense (first-person plural) meaning 'we were going' or 'we went/continued to go.' Col is the contraction of con + il ('with the'). Sol here means 'sun' (poetic), novo = 'new' i.e. newly risen; a le reni = 'to the loins' (literally) or 'toward the flanks/slopes'—a spatial phrase introduced by a.
segments.purgatorio-19-g53: Seguendo is a present gerund (following); it attaches an adverbial participle phrase to the main clause. Lui is the clitic/strong pronoun 'him' (object of seguendo). The phrase functions as a circumstantial modifier: 'following him.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g54: Portava is the imperfect tense (first- or third-person singular depending on context) of portare meaning 'bore/held'—here Dante as narrator 'bore' his forehead. 'La mia fronte' is the direct object ('my brow/forehead'). 'Come colui che l'ha di pensier carca' is a simile: colui 'he who', che = relative 'who', l' = pronoun referring to 'fronte', ha = present of avere 'has', 'di pensier carca' = 'laden with thought' (carca agrees with fronte).
segments.purgatorio-19-g55: The relative clause continues: che = 'who/that', fa = present of fare 'makes' or 'forms', 'di sé' = 'of himself', un mezzo arco di ponte = 'a half-arch of a bridge' (metaphor for bending over). The clause explains the posture compared above.
segments.purgatorio-19-g56: Quand' is the contracted form of quando 'when'. Io is the subject pronoun 'I' (explicit for emphasis). Udi' is passato remoto of udire 'I heard'. 'Venite' is the imperative or direct reported utterance 'Come (pl.)'. The whole is 'when I heard “Come...”'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g57: The quoted phrase 'qui si varca' uses the impersonal/reflexive si with varca (present indicative) to mean 'here one crosses' or 'here one may pass.' Parlare (here functioning as a participial/infinitival adverbial) describes how the words were said: 'spoken in a gentle and benign manner.' So the phrase links the utterance to its manner: 'spoken in a gentle and benign way.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g58: Qual = 'such as' or 'of a kind that'; non si sente = impersonal/passive 'is not heard' (si + sente); in questa mortal marca = 'in this mortal march/land/realm' (marca = archaic 'borderland' or 'realm'). The clause says the sweetness/kindness of that speech is not heard in this mortal world.
segments.purgatorio-19-g59: Con = 'with'; l'ali is the article + noun 'the wings' (l' = il elided before a vowel); aperte is the past participle/adjective 'open' agreeing in gender/number with ali. The phrase is a circumstantial prepositional phrase describing manner.
segments.purgatorio-19-g60: Che parean = 'which seemed' (parean is imperfect of parere, third plural). 'Di cigno' = 'of (a) swan' = like a swan's; the relative clause describes the wings' appearance. Note the imperfect gives a descriptive, continuous sense.
segments.purgatorio-19-g61: Volseci is a poetic/archaic verb form combining volse (passato remoto of volgere or volgere/volgere-derived form) with the enclitic ci 'us' meaning 'he turned us' or 'he turned toward us/upwards'; in sù = 'upward'. 'Colui che sì parlonne' = 'he who thus spoke' (colui = that one, che = who, parlonne an archaic past of parlare meaning 'spoke thus'). The final prepositional phrase locates the action 'between two faces/walls of the hard rock.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g62: Mosse is passato remoto (or poetic past) of muovere 'he moved'. Le penne = 'the feathers' (direct object). Poi = 'then/afterwards.' E = 'and.' Ventilonne is an archaic/poetic verb form (from ventilare) meaning 'he fanned' or 'he set to ventilating'; its conjugation is irregular/poetic here.
segments.purgatorio-19-g63: 'Qui lugent' is a Latin phrase included in the Italian line: lugent (Latin, 3rd pl.) = 'they mourn here.' Affermando is a gerund 'affirming/declaring'; esser is the infinitive 'to be'; beati is an adjective 'blessed' (here predicative of esser). The clause reports the assertion that 'they are blessed.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g64: Ch' (che) = 'that'; avran is future indicative (third plural) of avere: 'they will have' or 'they will be obliged/have to'; di consolar = a periphrastic infinitival construction 'to console'; l'anime = 'the souls' (direct object), donne = 'women' (modifies anime: 'souls of women'). The clause gives the content of the earlier affirmation.
segments.purgatorio-19-g65: "Che hai che pur inver' la terra guati?" is an idiomatic interrogative. "Che hai che" (= "what have you that.../why...") uses avere (hai, 2nd sing. present) in a causal idiom asking for the reason; "pur" = still; "inver'" (archaic contraction of in verso) means toward/against; "la terra" is the object of the direction phrase; "guati" is 2nd person singular present of guatare (to watch/keep watch), so the whole line asks why you are still watching toward the earth.
segments.purgatorio-19-g66: Punctuation: the comma marks the reported speech break and is not analyzed grammatically.
segments.purgatorio-19-g67: "la guida mia incominciò a dirmi," — "la guida mia" (article + noun + possessive) = "my guide"; "incominciò" is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of incominciare = "began"; "a dirmi" is the preposition a + infinitive dire with the enclitic object pronoun attached (dirmi = "to tell me").
segments.purgatorio-19-g68: "poco amendue da l'angel sormontati." — "poco" here = "shortly/a little"; "amendue" (archaic) refers to "both" (the two people/figures mentioned); "da" with the past participle expresses the agent/point from which something happened (here "by/from the angel"); "sormontati" is plural past participle of sormontare (= "surmounted/overcame/towered over"), agreeing with the two persons.
segments.purgatorio-19-g69: "E io:" = "And I:" — simple coordination "E" (and) followed by the subject pronoun "io" (I), introducing the speaker's reply.
segments.purgatorio-19-g70: The clause is inverted: "Con tanta sospeccion" (prepositional phrase, "with such suspicion/apprehension") modifies the action; "fa" (3rd sg.) = "makes/causes"; "irmi" is an adjective root + enclitic pronoun (irmo + mi) meaning "makes me solitary/withdrawn" (Dante uses fa + adjective + enclitic to mean "makes me X"); "novella visïon" is the subject ("a new vision") placed after the verb for poetic word order; "ch'a sé mi piega" is a relative clause: "that (ch') to itself (a sé) bends me (mi piega)" — "mi piega" = "bends/turns me (toward it)".
segments.purgatorio-19-g71: "sì ch'io non posso dal pensar partirmi." — "sì ch'" = "so that" (introducing a result clause); "io non posso" = "I cannot" (present indicative of potere); "dal" = da + il = "from the" and governs the noun/infinitive phrase "pensar" (thinking); "partirmi" is the infinitive partire with the enclitic pronoun mi (to part/withdraw oneself), so the phrase means "I cannot withdraw myself from thinking" i.e. "I cannot stop thinking."
segments.purgatorio-19-g72: "Vedesti," here is the 2nd person singular passato remoto of vedere (you saw/you perceived) addressed to the listener; it reports that the addressee has already seen what follows.
segments.purgatorio-19-g73: "disse," = 3rd person singular passato remoto of dire (he said); it tags the preceding verb as reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-19-g74: "quell'antica strega che sola sovr' a noi omai si piagne;" — "quell'antica strega" = "that ancient witch/enchanter" (demonstrative + adjective + noun); "che" introduces the relative clause; "sola" (= alone, only) emphasizes that she alone performs the action; "sovr' a noi" (sopra a noi) = "over/above us"; "omai" = "by now/now"; "si piagne" = reflexive present "she laments/complains" (si = reflexive pronoun, piagne = 3rd sg. present of piagnere/piagnire = to lament).
segments.purgatorio-19-g75: "vedesti come l'uom da lei si slega." — again "vedesti" = "you saw"; "come" = "how/as" introducing the subordinate clause; "l'uom" = "the man/people"; "da lei" = "from her"; "si slega" is pronominal present of slegare (to unbind/release), meaning "he frees/unties himself (from her)" — the reflexive si indicates the subject releases himself from her influence.
segments.purgatorio-19-g133: Quel che ... fa = a neuter demonstrative + relative clause meaning “what/that which ... does.” ch’ is the contracted che (that/which). Avarizia is the subject and fa is present indicative, 3rd person singular (does/makes). The whole segment functions as the clause’s subject.
segments.purgatorio-19-g134: qui si dichiara uses the impersonal/passive si (si + verb) with dichiara (present indicative) to mean “is declared/revealed here.” in purgazion is an archaic spelling for in purgation (in the purification), de l’ anime is “of the souls” (de + l’), and converse is an adjective meaning “turned” or “turned toward” (describing the souls).
segments.purgatorio-19-g135: e nulla pena il monte ha più amara is inverted order: nulla pena (“no punishment”) is the noun phrase, il monte ha = “the mountain has,” and più amara is a comparative predicate adjective (“more bitter”), feminine to agree with pena. The sense: “the mountain has no punishment more bitter.”
segments.purgatorio-19-g136: Sì come = “just as/just so.” l’occhio nostro (the eye of ours) is the subject; non s’aderse is past (passato) with the reflexive clitic si + aderse (archaic/passé form of aderger/adergere) meaning “did not adhere/cling.” in alto is an adverbial phrase “upwards/high.” The verb form is archaic but functions as a 3rd-person singular past action of the eye.
segments.purgatorio-19-g137: fisso is a past participle/adj meaning “fixed” (i.e. made steady); a le cose terrene = “on/to the earthly things,” with the archaic separation a + le (modern alle). This is a descriptive complement tied to l’occhio: it was fixed on worldly things.
segments.purgatorio-19-g138: così links the comparison: “thus/so.” giustizia is the subject (divine justice), qui is “here,” a terra is a locative/comparative phrase meaning “to the ground/downward,” il is the masculine clitic pronoun referring back to l’occhio, and merse is the passato remoto of mergere/mergere (to plunge/immerse) used figuratively. Word order places the object clitic before the verb (typical).
segments.purgatorio-19-g139: Come = “as/just as.” avarizia is the subject and spense is passato remoto of spegnere (“extinguished/quenched”), with a ciascun bene marking “with respect to each good” or “for every good.” lo nostro amore is the direct object (“our love”), with the archaic article lo before nostro. The clause says that avarice put out our love toward every good.
segments.purgatorio-19-g140: onde is a connective adverb meaning “whence/therefore/from which,” introducing the result clause. operar functions as a noun/infinitive meaning “to do (works/deeds)” — i.e. good actions — and perdési is an archaic past form meaning they/we lost or ceased (past tense). Together: “whence we lost the ability to do (good) works.”
segments.purgatorio-19-g141: così introduces the parallel: “so/thus.” giustizia is again the subject (divine justice), qui “here,” stretti is a participial adjective meaning “tight/closely bound,” ne is a clitic pronoun referring to “us” (of us/them) used partitively/accusatively, and tene is the verb (tenere) in present tense meaning “holds/keeps.” Thus: “so justice holds us here tight.” Note: ne + tene = clitic construction referring back to the souls.
segments.purgatorio-19-g142: ne’ piedi e ne le man = “in the feet and in the hands,” with ne (in) repeated for emphasis and the archaic separation ne’ = in + the. legati e presi are past participles used adjectivally: legati = “tied/bound,” presi = “seized/held,” describing how feet and hands are bound.
segments.purgatorio-19-g143: e quanto fia piacer del giusto Sire = “and as much/as long as it shall be the pleasure of the just Lord.” fia is an archaic/future subjunctive form of essere (“will be” / “may be”), piacer is the noun “pleasure/favor,” del giusto Sire = “of the just Lord” (God). The clause sets the duration: according to God’s pleasure.
segments.purgatorio-19-g144: tanto = “so much/that much” used adverbially to mirror quanto ... fia; staremo is the future first-person-plural of stare (“we shall remain/stay”), and immobili e distesi are predicate adjectives: immobili = “motionless,” distesi = “stretched out/lying stretched.” Together: “so shall we remain motionless and stretched out.”
segments.purgatorio-19-g145: Io m’era inginocchiato uses the imperfect (era + participle) to describe a past state 'I had been kneeling'; m’ is the reflexive/clitic for 'myself'; e volea dire = 'and I wished to say' with volea (imperfect of volere) indicating intention.
segments.purgatorio-19-g146: ma 'but' introduces the contrast; com’ io cominciai 'as I began' (com’ = come/as); ed el s’accorse = 'and he perceived it/realized it' — s’ is the reflexive clitic and accorse is passato remoto used here with si accorse = 'he noticed/realized'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g147: solo ascoltando is an adverbial phrase 'by merely listening' (solo = only/merely; ascoltando = gerund 'listening'), explaining how he perceived Dante's reverence.
segments.purgatorio-19-g148: del mio reverire = 'of my reverence' or 'regarding my reverential demeanor' (del = di + il; mio = my; reverire = noun/infinitive used substantively here meaning reverence/awe).
segments.purgatorio-19-g149: «Qual cagion» is an interrogative noun phrase 'What cause/reason...' with Qual 'what' modifying cagion 'cause'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g150: disse is passato remoto 'he said', introducing the direct question that follows.
segments.purgatorio-19-g151: in giù così ti torse is the quoted question 'what has so turned you downward?' — in giù = 'downwards', così = 'thus/so', ti is the clitic 'you' (direct object), and torse is passato remoto of torcere used figuratively 'twisted/turned' (i.e., caused you to bend down).
segments.purgatorio-19-g107: 'Chi fosti' is a past question: chi = 'who'; fosti is an archaic/past form (here used as a past-tense predicate 'were'); 'e perché' = 'and why'. 'vòlti avete i dossi al sù' uses a past participle before the auxiliary (vòlti + avete) — a poetic inversion of avete voltato — meaning 'you have turned'; i dossi = 'the backs/ridges' (dosso = back) and 'al sù' = 'upward/skyward'. The whole clause asks who the addressee was and why they have turned their backs upward.
segments.purgatorio-19-g108: mi dì is an imperative request: dì = imperative 'say/tell'; mi is the clitic object 'to me' (indirect object position) attached before the verb for economy. The comma marks a brief pause before the following conditional clause.
segments.purgatorio-19-g109: e se vuo' = 'and if you want' (vuo' = contracted present 'you want'). 'ch'io t'impetri' = che + subjunctive: ch'io = 'that I', impetri = present subjunctive of impetrare ('obtain by entreaty' / 'ask for on someone's behalf'), with the clitic t' = 'to you' (for you). 'cosa di là ond' io vivendo mossi' = 'something from over there which I set in motion while living': di là = 'from over there', ond' = 'whence/from which', vivendo = gerund 'while living', mossi = passato remoto 'I moved/set in motion'. The clause offers to entreat/obtain on their behalf something stemming from what the speaker put in motion in life.
segments.purgatorio-19-g110: Ed = and (variant of e). elli here functions as the subject 'he' (the speaker of the next words); 'a me' = 'to me', indicating that he addressed Dante. The whole chunk marks direct speech introduced by 'he to me'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g111: Perché introduces the content clause 'why ...'; i nostri diretri = 'our rears/backs' (diretro = rear, 'the rear parts belonging to us'). Rivolga is a present subjunctive/poetic form of rivolgere ('to turn'); here it expresses the content of the question ('why heaven turns our rear toward itself'). il cielo a sé = 'the heaven/sky toward itself' (sé = reflexive 'itself').
segments.purgatorio-19-g112: saprai is the future indicative second person singular of sapere: 'you will know' (a promise that the reason will be revealed).
segments.purgatorio-19-g113: ma prima = 'but first'; scias is the present subjunctive of sapere used jussively (an exhortation: 'know' / 'be aware'); quod = 'that'; ego fui = 'I was' (fui = passato remoto of essere); successor Petri = 'the successor of Peter' (i.e. a pope). Grammatically this is a parenthetical imperative: 'but first know that I was Peter's successor.'
segments.purgatorio-19-g114: Intra = 'between' (here indicating location between two places). Sïestri e Chiaveri are place names. s'‑adima is a reflexive/poetic expression: s' = si (reflexive/clitic) and Adimà is the river's name, so the construction conveys 'there is a river called Adimà' (the reflexive marks the naming). una fiumana bella = 'a fair/large river'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g115: e del suo nome = 'and from its name' (del = di + il). lo titol del mio sangue = 'the title of my blood/lineage' (lo titol = the title; del mio sangue = of my blood, i.e. family/line). fa sua cima = literally 'makes its summit' — idiomatically 'takes/has its crest (or highest point)'; the phrase means the family's title takes its highest point or origin from the river's name.
segments.purgatorio-19-g116: Un mese e poco più = 'a month and a little more' (time expression). prova' io is the contracted poetic form of provai io (passato remoto) = 'I experienced/found'; come pesa = 'how it weighs' (come = 'how'); il gran manto = 'the great cloak/mantle'. a chi dal fango il guarda = 'to one who, from the mud, looks at it' (a chi = 'to him who', dal fango = 'from the mud', il = object clitic referring back to manto, guarda = 'looks/watches').
segments.purgatorio-19-g117: che introduces a result/explanatory clause: 'so that'. piuma = 'feather' (used figuratively). sembran is a reduced/poetic form of sembrano ('they seem'); tutte l'altre some = 'all the other sums/loads' (some = old/plural form meaning sums/loads). The clause states that, by comparison, all other loads seem like feathers.
segments.purgatorio-19-g162: Se mai is a conditional opening ('if ever'); quel santo evangelico suono is a noun phrase ('that holy Gospel sound/passage') with the demonstrative quel modifying santo evangelico suono. The relative clause che dice 'Neque nubent' ('which says ...') follows the noun, and intendesti is a second-person singular past tense (past/perfect/remote) of intendere meaning 'you understood/heard' (an archaic past form).
segments.purgatorio-19-g163: Ben puoi veder is an idiom meaning 'you can well see' (ben reinforces puoi veder). Perch' is the apocopated form of perché ('why/for which reason') and io così ragiono is a present-tense main clause ('I thus reason' / 'why I argue so'), with ragiono in the present indicative.
segments.purgatorio-19-g164: Vattene is the imperative form va' + te (the reflexive/clitic te attached) meaning 'go away' (the enclitic -te is required with this verb); omai is an adverb meaning 'now' / 'by now'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g165: Non vo' che più t'arresti uses vo' (short for voglio) with non to mean 'I do not want'; the following che introduces a clause taking the present subjunctive arresti (2nd person singular) — a standard Italian construction 'non voglio che tu ...' meaning 'I don't want you to ...'. Più here means 'any longer'; t' is the clitic for 'te' (you).
segments.purgatorio-19-g166: Ché = perché ('because/for'), introducing a reason clause. La tua stanza is an expression where stanza (here archaic) means 'your stay/delay' (subject of the clause); mio pianger is a genitive construction with the infinitive used as a noun ('my weeping'), and disagia is a third-person singular present verb meaning 'it displeases' or 'causes discomfort' (the subject 'la tua stanza' displeases 'mio pianger').
segments.purgatorio-19-g167: Col qual is short for con il quale ('with which'); maturo here is used as a verb in the first-person singular present ('I ripen / I bring to maturity'), so the speaker says that by that (his weeping) he matures something. Ciò che tu dicesti is a relative clause 'that which you said', with dicesti in a past (remote) tense 'you said'.
segments.purgatorio-19-g168: Nepote ho io is an inverted subject-verb order for emphasis: 'I have a nephew/niece' (nepote here = niece/nephew). Di là means 'over there'; c'ha is a contracted relative (che ha) meaning 'who has' and nome Alagia is the idiom 'has the name Alagia' = 'is called Alagia'. The proper name Alagia is a personal name.
segments.purgatorio-19-g169: Buona da sé is a short adjectival phrase: buona ('good') qualifies the niece, and da sé ('by herself' / 'in herself') is an adverbial phrase indicating intrinsic goodness.
segments.purgatorio-19-g170: Pur che here functions as a concessive/conditional conjunction ('provided that' / 'so long as') and governs a subjunctive: la nostra casa non faccia lei ... malvagia = 'our household does not make her wicked'. Casa in this context means 'household/lineage/family', faccia is present subjunctive (3rd sing.) of fare used causatively, and per essempro is an archaic phrasing 'by/example' (per esempio).
segments.purgatorio-19-g171: E questa sola di là m’è rimasa has the simple coordination 'and this one alone'; di là repeats 'from/over there'. M' is the clitic mi ('to me'), contracted before the vowel; è is the auxiliary 'is' and rimasa is the past participle (archaic spelling) agreeing with questa, yielding 'remains to me' or 'is left to me'.

sentences.purgatorio-19-s9: He said fiercely; and he was coming with his eyes fixed still on that virtuous woman.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s10: The other seized her, and before him he would open her, splitting the draperies, and showed me the belly; that (sight) woke me with the stench that came out of it.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s11: I moved my eyes, and the good master said: 'At least three times I have called you!'
sentences.purgatorio-19-s12: He was saying, 'Rise and come; let us find the opening through which you enter.'
sentences.purgatorio-19-s5: I turned Ulysses from his wandering path toward my song; and whoever dares to be with me seldom parts from it; thus I satisfy him completely!
sentences.purgatorio-19-s7: His mouth had not yet been closed, when a holy and ready woman appeared to me, meanwhile, to make that one abashed.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s25: O elect of God, whose sufferings both justice and hope make less harsh, direct us toward the lofty ascents.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s26: If you have come safely from your lying (from bed), and wish to find the path more quickly, let your right hands always be on the outside.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s27: Thus the poet prayed, and an answer was given to us shortly afterward; because of that, as I spoke I warned the other who was hidden, and I turned my eyes to my lord's eyes: whereupon he consented to me with a cheerful nod to what the longing's sight was asking.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s28: After I was able to bring myself to my wits, that creature whose words had earlier made me take note drew me up onto it, saying: 'Spirit in whom it is fitting to weep that one without whom you cannot return to God, stop a little for my greater care of you.'
sentences.purgatorio-19-s34: It was late; but, when I had become a Roman shepherd, thus I discovered the deceitful life.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s35: I saw that there my heart would not be calmed, nor could I climb any more in that life; therefore love for this was kindled in me.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s36: Until that point I was a wretched soul separated from God; entirely greedy; now, as you see, here I am punished for it.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s1: At the hour when the daytime heat can no longer warm the chill of the moon, subdued by the earth, and at times by Saturn — when geomancers behold their Greater Fortune in the East, before dawn, rising along a road that soon grows dim to it — there came to me in a dream a stammering woman, one-eyed in the eyes, with her feet twisted, with maimed hands, and of a sallow complexion.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s2: I watched her; and as the sun comforts the cold limbs that the night burdens, so my gaze made language its escort for her, and afterwards in a little while it straightened her completely, and, as love wills, thus it colored her bewildered face.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s3: After she had her speech so loosened, she began to sing so that I, with difficulty, would have turned my intent away from her.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s4: “I am,” she sang, “I am sweet serenity, who relieves mariners in mid-sea of their distress; so full of pleasure am I to be heard!”
sentences.purgatorio-19-s21: Make yourselves ready, and stamp your heels on the ground; turn your eyes to the lure that the eternal King turns with his mighty wheels.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s22: As the falcon, which first watches at his feet, then turns to the call and stretches himself because of the longing for the prey that draws him there, so did I; and likewise, just as the rock splits to give way to one who goes upward, I went on until where the circling is taken.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s23: When I was let out into the fifth circuit, I saw people along it who were weeping, lying on the ground with their faces turned downward.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s24: "Adhaesit pavimento anima mea," I heard them say, with such deep sighs that the words could hardly be understood.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s42: And I to him: "Because of your dignity my conscience rightly reproached me."
sentences.purgatorio-19-s44: , answered; "Do not err: I am a guardian with you and with the others under one authority."
sentences.purgatorio-19-s13: I rose up; and already all the circuits of the sacred mountain were full of the high day, and we went with the newly risen sun toward the loins (flanks).
sentences.purgatorio-19-s14: Following him, I bore my brow as one who has it laden with thought, who makes of himself a half-arch of a bridge; when I heard ‘Come; here one crosses’ spoken in a gentle and benign manner, such as is not heard in this mortal land.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s15: With wings open, which seemed those of a swan, he turned us upward—he who had spoken thus—between two faces of the hard rock.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s16: He then moved his feathers and fanned them; saying ‘Here they mourn,’ he declared that they are blessed, who will have to console the souls of women.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s17: Why are you still staring down toward the earth?
sentences.purgatorio-19-s18: , my guide began to say to me, shortly after both had been overtaken by the angel above.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s19: And I: "This new vision fills me with such apprehension and draws me to itself, so that I cannot tear myself away from thinking."
sentences.purgatorio-19-s20: "You saw," he said, "that ancient witch who alone now laments over us; you saw how man frees himself from her."
sentences.purgatorio-19-s37: What avarice does is revealed here in the purging of the souls that were turned (toward earthly things); and the mountain has no punishment more bitter.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s38: Just as our eye did not cling upward but remained fixed on earthly things, so justice here plunged it down to the ground.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s39: Just as avarice extinguished our love for every good — hence we lost the power to do good works — so justice keeps us here tightly bound, our feet and hands tied and seized; and for as long as it pleases the just Lord, so shall we remain motionless and stretched out.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s40: I had knelt down and meant to speak; but as I began and he perceived it — merely by listening to my reverence — he said, 'What cause has so turned you downward?'
sentences.purgatorio-19-s29: Who were you, and why have you turned your backs upward? Tell me; and if you wish that I entreat for you something from over there which I set in motion while living.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s30: And he to me: 'You shall know why heaven turns our rear toward itself; but first know that I was Peter's successor.'
sentences.purgatorio-19-s31: Between Sestri and Chiavari a fair river called Adimà, and from its name the title of my lineage takes its crest.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s32: For a month and a little more I experienced how heavy the great mantle is to one who looks at it from the mud, so that all the other loads seem feathers.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s45: If you have ever understood that holy Gospel passage which says 'Neque nubent', you can well see why I reason thus.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s46: Go away now: I do not want you to stay any longer; for your staying displeases my weeping, with which I ripen what you said.
sentences.purgatorio-19-s47: I have a niece over there who is called Alagia, good in herself—provided that our household does not, by example, make her wicked; and this one alone remains to me over there.
